1.2 Mission statement: a more specific view (or statement) of how the company might contribute to its vision.

(ii) What is the company's approach to product & technology development
Example: World Leader in CN Technologies, based on advanced R&D, new product development, & acquisitions

extracted_json.summary_topic_line: Step 3 of the SPSP: building a structural (Five Forces) map of the PC industry in the 1980s.
extracted_json.summary_gist: This page covers Step 3 of the SPSP (Execute the plan), specifically Step 1: creating a structural map, illustrated with the Personal Computer Industry of the 1980s. The diagram maps the competitive forces around central Competitors (F1: Rivalry between Competitors), including New entrants (Compaq, NCR) with F2 threat of new entrants and barriers to entry; Suppliers (microprocessors—Intel, AMD; operating systems—Microsoft; storage—Seagate) with F4 supplier power; Buyers (consumers, enterprises) with F5 buyer power; Substitutes (pen computing—GO, Apple; PDA—Palm) with F3 threat of substitutes; Complementors (Intel) with F6 power to influence; and the existing PC competitors (IBM, HP, Dell, Apple). Agenda for TIM 172A Lecture #5 (10/9/25): a note on Complementors (not Complimentors), Company Analysis, and HW #2 Problem 2 (Intel). HW #2 is now strictly due Friday 10/10/25 at 11:59 pm, and HW #3 has been posted and will be strictly due on time Thursday 10/16/25. Notes introduce "Complementors" as a sixth set of players added to Porter's Positioning (Competitive Strategy) framework, giving rise to a sixth force: "power to influence." A health-care example illustrates this: pharmaceutical companies (e.g., Pfizer) compete in the medical drug delivery industry, while doctors act as complementors. Introduces company analysis for technology firms, noting the relationship between industry (e.g., microprocessor industry) and company (e.g., Intel) analysis. States that all successful companies have a vision statement (a high-level idea about the future, e.g., Microsoft's 1985 vision of computers in every home and desktop) and a mission statement (a more specific view of how the company contributes to its vision). Notes give Microsoft's ~1985 mission statement as an example, then list business goals (BGs) including annual sales revenue, growth, profit/net income, and profit margin (Net Income / Sales Rev. × 100%). It concludes that every tech company has three key strategies: competitive, technology (TS), and product/market (P/M s). Notes frame Technology Strategy as a set of questions paired with a CISCO example. (i) Identifies the core technologies giving a company a technological advantage over competitors—for CISCO, advanced state-of-the-art Computer Network (CN) technologies like routers and switches for a wide variety of customers. (ii) Asks about the company's approach to product and technology development—CISCO is a world leader in CN technologies, driven by advanced R&D, new product development, and acquisitions. This page introduces Product/Market Strategy (P/MS) through two guiding questions: (1) what differentiates the company's products from competitors, and (2) what markets the company serves. Using Cisco as an example, differentiators include reliability, security, and quality-of-service to customers. Cisco's market is segmented into enterprises (Fortune 100 companies), Internet Service Providers (ISPs like AT&T and Comcast), commercial (all other companies), and consumers. Notes on Product/Market Strategy organized as a two-column table (Definitions vs. Example using Cisco). Two key questions: (1) What differentiates the company's products from competitors — for Cisco, reliability, security, and quality-of-service to customers. (2) What markets does the company serve — Cisco segments its market into enterprises (Fortune 100), Internet Service Providers (AT&T, Comcast), Commercial (all other companies), and Consumers.
